evals.expected_output: A self-contained HTML5 game that teaches JavaScript closures through an interactive mechanic with 4-5 rounds, dark theme, gym tone, and a score/end screen.

assertions.text: Output is a single self-contained HTML file with no external script or stylesheet dependencies
Game has a visible round counter (e.g. Round X of Y)
Game has a visible score display
Game uses the dark color scheme (#09090b or similar dark background)
Game has 4 or 5 playable rounds
Correct answer feedback is visually distinct (green color or positive message)
Wrong answer feedback shows the correct answer with an explanation
Game has an end/completion screen with a score summary
Copy uses gym/training language (e.g. 'rep', 'round', 'locked in', 'good rep')
The challenges require reasoning about actual JavaScript closure code — not just definitions

evals.prompt: make a brainrot game about the JavaScript event loop and async/await
evals.expected_output: A self-contained HTML5 game teaching async execution order, ideally using the Execution Order mechanic, with 4-5 rounds, dark theme, gym tone, and end screen.

assertions.text: Output is a single self-contained HTML file with no external script or stylesheet dependencies
Game has a visible round counter
Game has a visible score display
Game uses the dark color scheme
Game has 4 or 5 playable rounds
Game mechanic involves ordering or sequencing (execution order), not just multiple choice
Wrong answer feedback shows the correct execution order with an explanation
Game has an end/completion screen with a score summary
Challenges involve real async/await or Promise code snippets

evals.prompt: I keep messing up array destructuring in JS. Can you make a game that drills me on it?
evals.expected_output: A self-contained HTML5 game that drills array destructuring through 4-5 rounds covering different patterns (basic, skip, rest, defaults), dark theme, gym tone, end screen.

assertions.text: Output is a single self-contained HTML file with no external script or stylesheet dependencies
Game has a visible round counter
Game has a visible score display
Game uses the dark color scheme
Game has 4 or 5 playable rounds
Rounds cover at least 3 distinct destructuring patterns (e.g. basic, rest, defaults, nested, skipping)
Wrong answer feedback shows the correct destructuring result with an explanation
Game has an end/completion screen with a score summary
